statement: Statistical learning is the process of estimating an unknown function 𝑓 from data to make predictions and uncover patterns.
Statistical models simplify complex real-world problems to make predictions and understand patterns.
Function fitting is a fundamental concept in machine learning model construction.
There is a trade-off between predictive accuracy and model interpretability in statistical learning.
Understanding probability and statistics principles is a prerequisite for statistical learning.
The bias-variance trade-off is a fundamental concept in statistical learning that needs to be mitigated using various techniques.
A row of X is written as x1, x2,…, xn, where xi is a vector of length p, containing p variable measurements for the ith observation.
Long format data has n observations greater than p variables, with all observations labelled.
In data analysis, a predictor variable is a feature used to predict the dependent variable.
Statistical learning focuses on understanding data, testing hypotheses, and explaining relationships.
Machine learning focuses on predicting outcomes, classifying data, and automating decisions.
Statistical learning methods include linear regression, logistic regression, LDA/QDA, Naïve Bayes, LASSO, and Ridge.
Machine learning methods include decision trees, random forest, Boosting methods, SVMs, CNNs, and RNNs.
Statistical learning models often rely on assumptions such as normality and independence.
Machine learning techniques are often assumption-free and focus on prediction accuracy and generalization.
Parametric Fitting assumes a fixed functional form (e.g., linear, cubic).
Non-Parametric Fitting makes minimal assumptions, letting data dictate shape.
Parametric Fitting has a small, fixed number of parameters.
Non-Parametric Fitting has a number of effective parameters that grows with data size.
Parametric Fitting works well with smaller datasets, while Non-Parametric Fitting requires larger datasets to avoid overfitting.
Parametric Fitting methods include linear regression, logistic regression, and polynomial regression.
Non-Parametric Fitting methods include KNN, kernel density estimation, splines, decision trees, and random forests.
Parametric Fitting has high interpretability, with coefficients having clear meaning (slope, intercept).
Non-Parametric Fitting has low interpretability, with complex structures harder to interpret.
Supervised Learning includes applications such as credit risk classification and detecting malignant cells in tissue samples.
All data in this context are unlabeled.
Small variations in DNA can be used to pinpoint someone’s place of origin to a remarkable degree of accuracy, often to within a few hundred kilometres.
Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where the goal is to discover hidden patterns and detect anomalies in unlabeled data.
Semi-supervised learning is a type of machine learning that combines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data to improve prediction.
Common unsupervised learning algorithms include k-Means, Gaussian Mixture Models, Hierarchical Clustering, DBSCAN, Spectral Clustering, PCA, ICA, t-SNE/UMAP, and Kernel Density Estimation.
Common semi-supervised learning algorithms include Linear/Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Trees, Random Forests, Gradient Boosting, k-NN, and Naive Bayes.
Unsupervised learning results may be hard to validate due to the lack of ground truth.
Semi-supervised learning can be limited by the expense and time required for labeling data.
The No Free Lunch theorem in statistical learning states that there is no single algorithm that can perform optimally across all possible problems.
Model error can be decomposed into variance, introduced by sampling, and bias, introduced by method selection.
Bias occurs when a model is too simple and ignores important relationships, leading to underfitting.
Variance occurs when a model is too complex and captures noise as if it were signal, leading to overfitting.
Lower bias usually increases variance, and lower variance usually increases bias.
Linear regression on highly non-linear data is an example of underfitting due to high bias.
A deep decision tree memorizing training data is an example of overfitting due to high variance.
To reduce bias, use more complex or flexible models, such as neural networks or ensembles.
To reduce variance, simplify the model, regularize, use more data, or prune the model.
Resampling helps reduce overfitting by evaluating variance on unseen subsets and comparing models in a fair, data-efficient way.
Sample without replacement: draw an observation, do not put it back, and ensure every observation can appear at most once in the sample.
Resampling provides more reliable estimation of model error and insight into model parameter accuracy statistics.
Basic Error in regression is the difference between predicted and actual values (residuals)
Core metrics for regression include MSE, RMSE, and MAE
MSE is sensitive to outliers and exaggerates large errors
Hold-Out CV involves splitting data into training and testing sets
k-Fold CV involves splitting data into k folds, training on k-1 folds, and validating on 1 fold, repeating k times
k-Fold CV offers a good bias-variance trade-off, especially when n is extremely large
Typically, 5 and 10 folds are used in k-Fold CV
Stratified k-Fold CV preserves class distribution in each fold
Leave-One-Out CV involves training on all data except 1 point and repeating for each point
Leave-One-Out CV uses maximum data for training but has low bias and high variance
Monte Carlo (Shuffle-Split) CV involves randomly splitting data into train/test sets multiple times and averaging results
Accuracy can be misleading with imbalanced data in classification problems
Basic Error in classification is the proportion of correctly classified samples (accuracy)
Core metrics for classification include precision, recall, and F1-score
Fitting a simpler model to complex data results in high bias and low variance.
Well Interpretable Model: Models whose inner workings and decisions can be easily understood by humans
Poorly Interpretable Model: Models that act as “black boxes,” where decision logic is complex or opaque
Well Interpretable Models include linear regression, logistic regression, shallow decision trees, Naïve Bayes
Poorly Interpretable Models include deep neural networks, ensemble methods (Random Forests, Gradient Boosting), Support Vector Machines with complex kernels
Well Interpretable Models have high transparency, where each feature’s contribution is explicit
Poorly Interpretable Models have low transparency, where predictions result from complex interactions across many parameters
Well Interpretable Models have higher bias and lower variance, making them stable but less accurate on complex data
Poorly Interpretable Models have lower bias and higher variance, making them powerful but risk of overfitting

Statistical learning relies on both predictors (features) and target variables to meet specific criteria.
The process of transforming raw, high-dimensional data into concise, informative features is a core part of statistical learning.
Limited working proficiency in Tidyverse dialect of R programming language is needed to complete practical exercises in statistical learning.
An attribute is a property or characteristic of an entity, often categorical or descriptive.
A variable is a measurable quantity that can take different values.
A predictor is a variable used specifically to explain or predict an outcome (dependent variable).
A feature is a representation of data used by algorithms, which can be raw variables or engineered transformations.
The layout of a dataset can affect its suitability for statistical learning, even if the data itself remains the same.
Every column is a variable, every row is an observation, and every cell is a single value in tidy data.
Tidy data is directly compatible with machine learning algorithms because predictors and outcomes are clearly separated.
Tidy data allows for straightforward feature engineering through transformations that apply cleanly to columns.
Tidy data enables clear mapping between variables and attributes, making it easier to explain model inputs and outputs.
Tidy data works seamlessly with data visualization libraries like seaborn, ggplot2, and matplotlib.
Tidy data enables analyses to be portable and consistent across tools and teams, promoting reproducibility.
Tidy data scales well to large datasets and allows pipelines to be automated.
Learning goal clarification involves defining the outcome to be modeled and identifying relevant independent variables.
Observations should be independent in regression analysis.
No severe multicolinearity is required in linear regression, polynomial regression, and poisson regression.
Homoscedasticity is required in linear regression and polynomial regression.
Large sample size is recommended for linear regression, polynomial regression, and poisson regression.
Logistic regression is used for binary target variables.
Feature scaling is required for LDA/QDA and Support Vector Machines.
Normality is not required for linear regression, polynomial regression, and poisson regression.
Model-agnostic feature selection methods include correlation, mutual information, and variance threshold.
LASSO and Elastic Net are examples of model-dependent feature selection methods.
Filter Methods evaluate features independently of the model using correlation, coefficient of determination, mutual information, Chi-squared, F-test, variance threshold.
Wrapper Methods use model performance to guide selection through forward selection, backward elimination, and stepwise selection.
Embedded Methods integrate feature selection into model training using techniques like LASSO, Elastic net, decision tree importance, and recursive feature elimination.
Domain-guided Methods apply expert knowledge or constraints to feature selection through manual curation, rule-based inclusion/exclusion, and clinical relevance filters.
Check if features are available, relevant, and measurable before selecting them.
Select features based on your needs and data, then choose a machine learning model after feature selection guided by Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA).
Ensure data quality by handling missing values, outliers, and inconsistencies before modelling.
Regularization, dimensionality reduction (PCA), and embedded feature selection (e.g., LASSO) can be applied after feature selection if needed.
Feature relevance supports manual selection based on domain knowledge and correlation analysis, and enables automatic selection via importance scores, mutual information, or embeddings.
Assumption checking is crucial for testing linearity, homoscedasticity, independence, and multicolinearity to focus on overfitting and bias-variance trade-off.
Visualization for stakeholders can communicate statistical relationships and diagnostics (e.g., residual plots) and feature impact and prediction behavior (e.g., SHAP, partial dependence).
Variables are mappings of data to visual dimensions
Geoms refer to the shape used in visualizations, such as points, lines, or bars
Aesthetics are visual attributes, including color, size, and shape
Stats are transformations or summaries of data, such as counts or smoothers
Coordinates refer to the space used in visualizations, including Cartesian or polar
Scales determine how values map to aesthetics, including linear, log, or categorical scales
Facets involve splitting data into subplots, combining visual and table data in one canvas
Tidy Data is a standardized way of organizing datasets where each variable is a column, each observation is a row, and each type of observation is a table.
A Grammar of Data Manipulation ensures experimental reproducibility in data science.
Feature Selection is the process of identifying the most relevant variables for modeling.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the process of examining and summarizing datasets to uncover patterns, spot anomalies, test assumptions, and generate insights before formal modeling.
The Grammar of Graphics is a conceptual framework for building statistical graphics, where charts are composed from a set of consistent “grammar rules” rather than predefined templates.
Linear regression is a statistical learning method that forms the foundation for many modern statistical learning methods.
Least squares was developed by Carl Friedrich Gauss for astronomical and geodetic problems.
Karl Pearson formalized regression and introduced the correlation coefficient.
R. A. Fisher integrated regression into the emerging field of modern statistics by connecting least squares, maximum likelihood, and analysis of variance.
With computational power, regression evolved into generalized linear models, regularized regression, nonparametric regression, and machine learning variants.
Francis Galton's study of the inheritance of human height found that children of very tall parents are on average slightly shorter, and children of very short parents tend to be taller.
Dependent variable (Feature) is the variable being predicted.
Machine learning includes supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and deep learning.
Examples of supervised learning tasks include credit risk classification, customer behavior prediction, and house price prediction.
Examples of unsupervised learning tasks include clustering, dimensionality reduction, and visualizing user behavior.
To predict future sales, consider the relationship between advertising budget and sales, the strength of the relationship, and the association between each medium and sales.
Linear regression is a model where the dependent variable is continuous and is predicted using a linear combination of predictor variables: y = β₀ + ∑βᵢxᵢ.
In R, linear regression can be implemented using the lm() function, e.g., lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data).
Logistic regression is a model where the dependent variable is binary and is predicted using a logistic function: y = 1 / (1 + e^(-z)), where z = β₀ + ∑βᵢxᵢ.
In R, logistic regression can be implemented using the glm() function with the binomial family, e.g., glm(y ~ x1 + x2, data, family = binomial()).
Poisson regression is a model where the dependent variable is a count and is predicted using a Poisson distribution: y ~ Poisson(λ), where ln(λ) = β₀ + ∑βᵢxᵢ.
In R, Poisson regression can be implemented using the glm() function with the poisson family, e.g., glm(y ~ x1 + x2, data, family = poisson()).
There is a goal to determine whether the data provide evidence of an association between advertising expenditure and sales.
The relationship between X and Y is assumed to be linear, approximately modeled as Y ≈ β0 + β1X + ε.
β0 and β1 are unknown constants that represent the intercept and slope of the linear relationship.
The goal of estimating coefficients is to find an intercept β0 and a slope β1 such that the resulting line is as close as possible to the n data points.
The residual sum of squares (RSS) is defined as the sum of the squared differences between observed and predicted values, RSS = ∑ei^2.
The least squares approach chooses β0 and β1 to minimize the RSS.
A model object is a wrapper object for a linear regression model, which includes coefficients such as 𝛽₀ and 𝛽₁.
Ordinary least squares normally gives coefficients through a precise linear-algebra solution, so no iterative search is required.
In real-world settings with massive datasets, streaming data, or strong regularization, computing the exact solution becomes impractical or unstable.
Iterative methods like Gradient Descent or Coordinate Descent become essential in certain niches because they scale better, handle constraints, and adapt to data that cannot be processed in one shot.
A loss function, also known as a cost function, is an error measure for a single training example, denoted as 𝐿(𝜃).
Examples of loss functions include Squared Error (L2), Absolute Error (L1), and Huber Loss.
An aggregated error measure for the entire dataset is denoted as 𝐽(𝜃) and provides a global objective to optimize model parameters.
Examples of aggregated error measures include RSS, AE, and Mean Squared Error.
Gradient Descent can be used when the dataset is huge, the matrix is too large to invert, or when using stochastic or mini-batch training.

Initialize the parameter (weight) values randomly, then partially differentiate the cost function with respect to different parameters, and update the parameters by an amount proportional to the gradient.
The learning rate parameter, 𝛼, is a vital hyperparameter in deciding how fast the parameters’ updates should happen.
A large learning rate may lead to drastic updates and gradient descent may diverge from the minima.
A smaller learning rate requires many updates, hence learning would be slow.
Gradient descent cannot run when the function is noisy, not differentiable, a black box, expensive to evaluate, or has discontinuities.
Coordinate search survives when gradient descent cannot run because it doesn't need derivatives.
Computing the full gradient vector might be too slow for models with billions of parameters or a cost function that requires huge computation per gradient.
Coordinate descent updates one parameter at a time, which can be much cheaper than computing the full gradient vector.
Certain optimization problems have a structure where each variable can be optimized independently or cheaply.
Gradient descent does not exploit the structure of optimization problems where each variable can be optimized independently or cheaply.
Coordinate descent shines in problems with a structure where each variable can be optimized independently or cheaply because each update is simple, fast, and often closed-form.
Many classical ML problems are sparse, meaning most variables are zero or irrelevant.
Gradient descent does a lot of unnecessary work and converges slower than alternative solutions in sparse problems.
Coordinate methods are faster and converge faster than gradient descent in sparse problems because they update only meaningful coordinates.
Gradient descent struggles when the landscape is ugly, such as when the surface is jagged or the function is not smooth.
Optimize one coordinate at a time by holding all other coordinates fixed and minimizing the objective with respect to the ith coordinate.
Update only the ith coordinate using the formula 𝜃[𝑖] =  𝜃[𝑖] − 𝛼 ∗ 𝐺 ௜.
The dependent variable is the variable being predicted or explained, while the predictor variable is the variable used to make predictions.
The null hypothesis (H0) states that there is no relationship between the predictor variable and the dependent variable, while the alternative hypothesis (H1) states that there is some relationship.
The p-value measures the probability of observing a t-value at least as extreme as the one observed, assuming that the true coefficient is zero.
The coefficient of determination (R²) measures the proportion of the variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the predictor variable.
R² = 1 - (RSS / TSS)
Simple regression analysis can be used to isolate the unique contribution of each medium to sales.
Omitted-variable bias occurs when predictors are correlated and each model ignores other predictors, resulting in biased coefficients.
Inflated significance can occur when predictors proxy for other variables, making them appear significant.
Multiple regression includes all predictors simultaneously, removing bias from omitted variables and providing a unified assessment of all predictors.
Multiple regression partitions shared variance properly and reveals collinearity through SE inflation and diagnostics.
The equation 𝑌 ≈ 𝛽 ଴+𝛽ଵ𝑋ଵ+𝛽ଶ𝑋ଶ+⋯+𝛽௣𝑋௣+𝜖 represents a multiple regression model.
Simple regression analysis can be used to estimate the relationship between a dependent variable (e.g., Sales) and a single independent variable (e.g., TV, Radio, Newspaper).

Collinearity refers to the phenomenon where two or more predictor variables in a regression model are highly correlated with each other, making it difficult to separate their individual contributions to the response variable.
Collinearity can lead to inflated standard errors, unstable estimates, and misleading p-values in regression analysis.
Interaction terms (e.g., TV × Radio) can be used to capture synergy or non-additivity between predictor variables in a regression model.
Regularization or variable selection techniques can be used to address collinearity and improve the stability of regression models.

Potential problems in regression analysis include non-linearity of the response-predictor relationships, correlation of the error terms, non-constant variance of error terms, outliers, high-leverage points, and collinearity.
The linear regression model assumes that there is a straight-line relationship between the predictors and the response.
Non-Linearity of the Response-Predictor Relationships can lead to suspect conclusions from the fit.
𝑚𝑝𝑔= 𝛽଴+𝛽ଵ×ℎ𝑜𝑟𝑠𝑒𝑝𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟 +𝛽ଶ×ℎ𝑜𝑟𝑠𝑒𝑝𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟ଶ
The linear regression model assumes that error terms 𝜖ଵ,𝜖ଶ,…,𝜖௡are uncorrelated.
Correlation of the Error Terms can lead to narrower confidence intervals and lower p-values than they should be.
Homoscedasticity: the linear regression model assumes that error terms have constant variance 𝑉𝑎𝑟𝜖௜= 𝜎ଶ.
Non-Constant Variance of Error Terms can affect the estimation of standard errors, confidence intervals and hypothesis testing.
An outlier is a point that is too far from the predicted value by the model.
High leverage points are those with leverage statistics exceeding p + 1 / n.
High leverage points have a substantial impact on least squares fit coefficients.
Variance inflation factor (VIF) is calculated as VIF = 1 / (1 - R^2).
Common rule-of-thumb thresholds for VIF are: > 5 for moderate collinearity and > 10 for severe collinearity.
Collinearity results in a decline of t-statistics and an increase in the standard error of regression coefficients.
Balance ~ Age + Limit is an example of a multiple regression model.
The RSE estimates the standard deviation of the response from the population regression line.
The R^2 statistic records the percentage of variability in the response that is explained by the predictors.
In multiple linear regression, low p-values for a predictor's t-statistic indicate a significant relationship with the response variable.
For the Advertising data, the p-values for TV and Radio are low, indicating a significant relationship with sales, while the p-value for Newspaper is not.
The standard error of a regression coefficient can be used to construct confidence intervals for the coefficient.
For the Advertising data, the 95% confidence intervals for the coefficients of TV and Radio are narrow and far from zero, providing evidence that these media are related to sales.
If the relationships between predictors and response variable are linear, then the residual plots should display no pattern.
Relaxing the assumption of linearity can be a simpler way to improve a linear model than reducing its complexity using techniques like ridge regression or the lasso.
Techniques for extending the linear model include polynomial regression, step functions, regression splines, smoothing splines, and local regression.

The steps to construct an ML model include understanding data, formulating a hypothesis, building the model, assessing its performance, generalizing the model, interpreting the results, and refining the model if necessary.
To construct an ML model, one needs to pick a modelling function, find its parameters, check data requirements, prove that the training data are representative, and determine model hyperparameters.
A link function is a special version of the Bayes classifier used to discriminate defaulters from non-defaulters.
A Gaussian probability modelling function (PDF) is used to model the value distribution of each predictor.
A discriminant score is relatively large if there is a high probability that an observation in the kth class has X≈x.
To discriminate defaulters from non-defaulters, we need a link function, a Gaussian probability modelling function (PDF), and a discriminant score.

FP (Type I error) is the error of incorrectly classifying a non-defaulter as a defaulter, while FN (Type II error) is the error of incorrectly classifying a defaulter as a non-defaulter.
Logistic regression is a model used for training and prediction.
The F1 score is used to evaluate the performance of a model.
A logistic regression model with a 0.5 cut-off for δ has a sensitivity of 0.51 and specificity of 0.98.
A lower model error indicates better model performance.
Logistic regression is used for classification problems.
Linear discriminant analysis is a classification method.
Model error is compared to baseline error to evaluate model performance.
More than one hyperplane can exist.
The value of each point gives us some idea how far from the separating hyperplane the point lies.
A maximal margin classifier is a classifier that maximizes the margin.
A support vector is a point that lies closest to the separating hyperplane.
Soft margin refers to a hyperplane that allows for some data points to be misclassified.
Using 2p features instead of p features can be done with a support vector classifier.

The tidyverse is a coherent, opinionated dialect of the R language that reshapes how data analysis and modelling are performed.
The tidyverse provides a unified syntax for data manipulation, visualization, and transformation.
The tidymodels framework extends the tidyverse principles to machine learning, offering a tidy grammar for preprocessing, resampling, tuning, and evaluating models.
The dplyr package implements a grammar of data manipulation, providing a consistent set of verbs to solve common data manipulation challenges.
The magrittr package allows you to combine pieces of code into clean, linear workflows using pipes (%>%).
The purrr package automates many tasks with functional programming.
Tidyr is a package that helps to format data according to the tidy principles, where each variable goes in a column, and each column is a variable.
The Reticulate package makes it seamless to integrate Python workflows directly within R.
Data visualization refers to the visual representations of data, such as charts, graphs, and dashboards, that help users quickly grasp complex information.
ggplot2 is a powerful system for creating graphics based on The Grammar of Graphics.
Plotly is a versatile library for creating interactive, web-based graphics in Python, R, and other languages.
The patchwork library in R is designed to make it incredibly simple to combine multiple ggplot2 plots into a single cohesive graphic.
Data analysis involves applying statistical and analytical techniques to the data to identify patterns, correlations, and trends.
The Tidymodels package in R enables users to perform complex calculations and build models without requiring extensive programming knowledge.
rsample provides functions for data splitting, which enables robust estimation of the sampling distribution of statistics such as model parameters or prediction error.
parsnip provides a tidy, unified interface to models, allowing users to try a range of models without getting bogged down in the syntactical minutiae of the underlying packages.
recipes enables users to use dplyr-like pipeable sequences of feature engineering steps to get their data ready for modeling.
workflows allows users to bundle together their pre-processing, modeling, and post-processing requests.
The Caret package in R offers a unified interface for building, tuning, and evaluating predictive models.
R Markdown is a powerful tool for creating interactive documents that combine R code, plots, and narrative text in a single file.
Shiny is an R package that lets users create web applications with interactive user interfaces.
To install a package in R, use the command install.packages("package_name").
To create a recipe containing a dependent variable plus two numeric predictors, then center and scale (normalize) the predictors, use the recipes package in R.
R gives priority to the most recently loaded package, which means its functions will take precedence over those from previously loaded packages.
Use the conflicts_prefer() function from the conflicted package to specify which version of a function you want to use.
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter) to prefer dplyr::filter over any other package.
An expression in R is a value or anything that evaluates to a value.
A statement in R is a unit of execution that does not have a return type.
Expressions in R can be used as part of other expressions.
The expression 1 + 2 is an example of an expression that returns the value 3.
The expression std::max(1.0, 2.5) is an example of an expression that returns the value 2.5.
The code ast(2*(a-1)) can be used to visualize the abstract syntax tree of an expression.
Statements in R are typically separated by a semicolon.
The code if(somethingIsTrue()) {doSomething() ;} is an example of a statement that contains other statements and expressions.
Base R offers a flexible, historically layered toolkit, while the tidyverse provides a modern, consistent grammar for data manipulation and visualization.
Every expression is a statement when it’s defined in R.
R can handle various simple math operators with ease, including +, -, *, /, and ^ or ** for exponentiation.
Exponentiation has the highest priority, followed by division, multiplication, subtraction, and addition in R.
Expressions are evaluated from left to right in R, but priority can be adjusted by using round brackets.
The expression (2*5)+1 evaluates to 11 in R.
Logical operators in R are used to perform logical operations and include relational operators and logical operators.
Relational operators in R include <, >, <=, >=, ==, and != and are used to determine the truth of a given expression.
The expression 2 < 5 evaluates to TRUE in R.
Special operators in R include assignment operators, concatenation operators, modular operators, sequence operators, and boolean inclusion operators.
The assignment operator <- is used to assign a value to a variable in R, e.g. x <- 5.
The <- operator creates a binding between the name on the left-hand side and the object on the right-hand side in R.
In R, when an object is modified, a new object is created with the modified value and the original object remains unchanged, a behavior known as copy-on-modify.
The code x <- c(1, 2, 3); y <- x; y[[3]] <- 4 demonstrates copy-on-modify in R.
The pipe-forward operator |> is syntactic sugar for chained method calls, letting you pass an intermediate result onto the next function.
The pipe-forward operator |> was introduced in R 4.1.
%>% is a pipe-forward implementation from the magrittr package.
The behaviour of the magrittr pipe %>% and the native pipe |> is by and large the same.
R normally resolves conflicts between functions with the same name by giving precedence to the function from the most recently loaded package.
To define the function you really want to call when there are conflicts, use the conflicts_prefer() function from the conflicted package, or the :: operator.
Atomic vectors in R are the fundamental building blocks, storing data of a single type, such as numeric, character, or logical.
The general pattern for vector initialization in R is vector(mode, length).
Create a numeric vector named x with the sequence of integers 1, 2, and 3 using x <- 1:3.
Factors are vectors containing nominal and ordinal data, derived from the integer class by enumerating a pre-defined set of values, called levels.
Create a factor f using f <- factor(c("Hot", "Hot", "Cold")) and print its levels using levels(f).
Use the table() function to summarize data counts for each level, such as table(f) to count the number of cold and hot instances in f.
One-hot encoding is a process that transforms categorical variables into numeric variables, often using the ifelse function or the case_when function.
Use the ifelse function to re-code "cold" as 0 and "hot" as 1, such as ifelse(f == "Cold", 0, 1).
Atomic vectors in R are limited to one type of elements.
The order of coercion in R is approximately: logical < integer < numeric < complex < character < list.
To explicitly define the type to which an object should be coerced, use functions that start with as.*() (e.g., as.logical(), as.integer(), as.double(), or as.character()).
Object attributes are dropped during the coercion process in R.
x <- c(1.7, "a"); names(x) <- c("Double", "Character"); x
In R, logical values are converted to numbers: TRUE is converted to 1 and FALSE to 0.
The typeof() function in R returns the internal type or storage mode of an object, whilst class() returns the object’s object-oriented classification according to the R class hierarchy.
In R, if two vectors are of the same length, arithmetic operations are done element-wise.
In R, if one vector is shorter than the other vector, R will recycle the shorter vector until it matches the length of the longer one.
A vector can have an infinite number of elements, and elements can be added or removed using functions such as c() or append().
c(x, 10) appends 10 to the vector x.
There are six ways to subset an atomic vector in R.
The square bracket operator [ is used for vector subsetting in R.
x[x < 2 | x == 3] subsets the vector x to include only elements less than 2 or equal to 3.
x[c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)] restricts the vector x using a logical vector.
x[c(1, 3)] subsets the vector x to include only the elements at indices 1 and 3.
The [ operator can be used to subset an atomic vector with character indices.
x[2] <- 20 replaces the second element of the vector x with 20.
replace(x, 1, 10) replaces the first element of the vector x with 10.
NA represents Not Applicable and NaN represents Not a Number in R, used for missing data.
Missing values in R are infectious, meaning most computations involving a missing value will yield another missing value.
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) calculates the mean of the vector x, ignoring missing values.
na.omit(x) removes missing values from the vector x.
Rectangular data refers to a two-dimensional array of values that are organized into rows and columns.
Each row in rectangular data typically represents a different observation or record, while each column represents a different variable or feature.
There are two basic forms of rectangular data: long data and wide data.
An array is the simplest rectangular data type, created by folding an atomic vector into multiple dimensions.
To create an array in R, use the code x <- 1:8; dim(x) <- c(2, 4); x
Arrays in R are atomic vectors folded into two or more dimensions.
To fold an atomic vector into an array with 2 rows and 4 columns in R, use the code dim(x) <- c(2, 4); x
To name rows and columns of an array in R, use the code dimnames(x) <- list(c("Alpha", "Beta"), c("Gamma", "Delta", "Epsilon", "Zeta")); x
A matrix is a collection of elements of the same data type arranged into a fixed number of rows and columns.
To create a matrix from a vector in R, use the code matrix(x, nrow = 4)
Matrices are often replaced by data frames in modern data analysis because matrices are strictly two-dimensional and can only contain one type of data.
To build a matrix by row-binding atomic vectors in R, use the code m <- rbind(m, rnorm(n = 3, mean = i, sd = 0.5))
The natural behavior of rbind() in R is row-binding, while cbind() is used for column-binding.
An array is a homogeneous data structure that can contain greater than or equal to 1 dimensions.
A matrix is a homogeneous data structure that contains 2 dimensions in a table-like structure.
Arrays are a superset of matrices, and matrices are a subset or special case of arrays where the dimensions attribute is equal to two.
The array() function can be used to create arrays by specifying the third dimension to be 1, while the matrix() function can be used to create at most 2-dimensional arrays.
Integer subsetting of arrays uses element indices, and logical subsetting checks the validity of an expression for each element and keeps elements that are evaluated as TRUE.
m[c(1,3),1:2] is an example of integer subsetting, where m is a matrix and c(1,3) are the row indices and 1:2 are the column indices.
The standard behavior of the [ operator on a matrix is to keep elements for which indices are provided.
The first position after [ applies to rows, the second to columns, and so on.
Leaving an empty slot in the [ operator will keep all elements of that dimension in the output, e.g., m[1,].
m < 2 is an example of logical subsetting, where m is a matrix and the expression m < 2 returns a Boolean vector of the same length as m.
The abind() function is an R package that generalizes cbind() and rbind() and works with vectors, matrices, and higher-dimensional arrays.
A list in R is an application of recursive vectors, where each element can be of a different mode, such as a list, character vector, or Boolean vector.

statement: All rows and columns in rectangular data must be of the same length.
Rectangular data is commonly stored in CSV, TSV, or TXT files, and Excel sheets.
Rectangular data can be imported into R using various functions from the readr and readxl packages.
Arrays are multi-dimensional generalizations of vectors capable of holding elements of the same type across multiple dimensions.
Lists are versatile data structures that can hold elements of different data types.
Tibbles are a modern version of data frames with enhanced features for data manipulation.
To import data into R, use the readr package and its functions, such as read_delim(), read_csv(), and read_tsv().
To set up a working directory in R, use the setwd() function, and to retrieve the current working directory, use the getwd() function.
read_csv() is a variant of the read_delim() function dedicated to reading comma-separated fields in files with a new line character (\n) separating lines.
The list.files() function generates a character vector containing the names of files and directories within a specified directory.
To customize the output of list.files(), use arguments such as pattern to filter files by name, all.files to include hidden files, and full.names to return the full paths.
list.files(include.dirs = FALSE, full.names = FALSE, all.files = FALSE) returns a list of file names in the current directory.
The read_csv() function imports data from a CSV file and stores it as a data frame.
data <- read_csv("players_22.csv") loads the players_22.csv file into a data frame called data.
The read_csv() function returns a tibble, while the read.csv() function returns a data frame.
To transpose a data frame, use the t() function.
t(read.csv("players_22.csv")) transposes the data frame loaded from the players_22.csv file.
The na parameter in the read_csv() function specifies which strings should be treated as missing values (NA) when importing data.
read_csv("players_22.csv", na = c("NA", "")) loads the players_22.csv file and treats both NA and empty strings as missing values.
The attach() function makes the variables in a loaded data frame accessible without having to reference the data frame explicitly.
The View() function opens a spreadsheet-style data viewer within RStudio.
To view a data frame in RStudio, use the View() function.
The head() function displays the first few rows of a data frame or tibble.
By default, head() shows the first 6 rows, but you can customize the number of rows displayed by specifying the n parameter.
The tail() function displays the last few rows of a data frame or tibble.
The slice() function from the dplyr package allows you to select specific row numbers to print from a tibble.
slice(data, c(1:3, 10, 20))
The slice() function makes it easy to extract and view precise subsets of your data frame.
A function in R is a set of statements that perform a specific task, which can be reused throughout the code, enhancing readability and maintainability.
To define a custom function in R, use the function() call followed by a set of parameters and a function body, such as my_function <- function(x, y) { result <- x + y }
Each function in R consists of three main components: function call, body, and environment.
The last line of a function in R inherently specifies the function's return value, so no additional syntax is needed.
To explicitly define the function's output in R, use the return() function in the last line of the function's body, such as return(length * width)
Named arguments in R allow you to specify the value of a particular parameter by its name, enhancing readability and reducing the chances of errors.
To call a function with named arguments in R, use the argument name followed by the value, such as area <- calculate_area(length = 10, width = 5)
Default arguments in R are predefined values for function parameters that are used if the user does not provide a specific value.
To define a function with default arguments in R, use the argument name followed by the default value, such as greet <- function(name, greeting = 'Hello') { return(paste(greeting, name)) }
In R, arguments are matched first by exact name, then by prefix matching, and finally by position.
The ... argument in R allows for an arbitrary number of arguments to be passed, which can be particularly useful in functions that are wrappers around other functions.
To capture the arguments passed through ... in R, use the list(...) function, such as capture_args <- function(...) { args <- list(...); return(args) }
Primitive functions are built-in functions implemented in C using the Primitive() function.
Anonymous functions are functions created on-the-fly without a name, defined using the (function(x) x+x) syntax.
Generic functions are functions that adapt their behavior based on the input type.
Primitive functions have high efficiency due to direct C implementation but limited flexibility and readability.
Anonymous functions are efficient for short-lived tasks and have high flexibility for temporary use cases.
To calculate the standard deviation, first compute the deviation of each value from the mean, then square each deviation, and finally take the square root of the mean of the squared deviations.

The formula for standard deviation is \( \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2} \), where \( \sigma \) is the standard deviation, \( N \) is the number of values, \( x_i \) represents each individual value, and \( \mu \) is the mean across all values.

To compute the standard deviation using intermediary objects, store the results of each analytic step in an object, such as out <- deviation(data$skill_dribbling), out <- square(out), out <- mean(out), and out <- sqrt(out).
Using the %>% operator from the magrittr package, the standard deviation can be calculated as data$skill_dribbling %>% deviation() %>% square() %>% mean() %>% sqrt().

The %>% operator places the left-hand side as the first argument in the right-hand side function call, while the |> operator places the left-hand side as the first argument by default but can be adjusted using the dot (.) placeholder.
In R, curly braces {} are not always necessary when defining functions, particularly for simple one-liners.
If the function body consists of a single expression, you can omit the curly braces in R.
Lexical scoping is a process of finding the value associated with a name, also known as static scoping.
R uses lexical scoping to determine the value associated with a name.
Name masking: names defined within a function take precedence over names defined outside the function.
If a name isn’t found inside a function, R looks one level up in the environment hierarchy.
x <- 10; f01 <- function() { x <- 20; x }; f01() returns 20
Functions are treated as ordinary objects in R, and the same scoping rules apply to them as well.
f04 <- function(x) x + 1; f05 <- function() { f04 <- function(x) x + 100; f04(10) }; f05() returns 110
In a function call, R will ignore non-function objects when searching for that name, ensuring the function is correctly identified and executed.
Each function call in R creates a new environment, which means the function has no memory of previous runs.
The function f08 checks if the variable a exists in the current environment using the exists("a") function.
In R, the evaluation of expressions is deferred until their values are actually needed, which is known as lazy evaluation.
An environment in R is a collection of objects like functions, variables, and data frames, which is essentially a space where R stores and manages these objects.
Lexical scoping in R determines where to look for values, but not when, which means the function’s output can vary based on objects outside its environment.
The function f09 <- function(x) {10} demonstrates lazy evaluation, as the argument x is not evaluated even if it contains an error, such as stop("This is an error!").
In R, environments are particularly important for function calls, as each function call creates a new environment that helps keep variables local to that function.
Every environment has a parent environment, except for the empty environment.
The global environment is where all interactive computations occur in R.
If a name is not found in an environment, R will search in its parent environment.
The parent environment of a function is where the function was created.
Environments are organized hierarchically, which is the foundation of R’s scoping rules.
Variables in the global environment are accessible from any function or script.
The grammar of data manipulation is a concept that provides a consistent set of functions to perform the most common data manipulation tasks and consists of verbs and nouns.
In the analogy of the grammar of data manipulation, the data frame can be thought of as the noun, while the functions are the verbs that perform the actions on the data.
Cross-sectional data is a type of statistical data that is collected at a single point in time.
Longitudinal data observes the same subject over a period of time, while cross-sectional data provides a quick overview of different subjects at the same point in time.
The FIFA dataset is an example of multivariate cross-sectional data.
Attributes, variables, features, and columns are interchangeable terms in the realm of machine learning, referring to the different aspects of data that can be measured or observed.
In traditional statistics, variables refer to the different aspects of data that can be measured or observed, while in machine learning, these are often referred to as features.
R provides five essential verbs to manipulate data: select, filter, arrange, mutate, and summarise.
The select() function in R picks columns based on their names, equivalent to subset(, select = ...) or data[, ...].
The filter() function in R picks rows that satisfy certain criteria, equivalent to subset() or data[... , ].
The arrange() function in R changes row ordering, equivalent to data[order(...), ].
The mutate() function in R creates a new column that is a function of existing columns, equivalent to transform() or direct assignment with $.
The summarise() function in R reduces multiple values down to a single summary, equivalent to aggregate(), tapply(), or by().
data%>%select(short_name, dob, skill_moves, skill_dribbling, skill_curve, skill_fk_accuracy, skill_long_passing, skill_ball_control)%>%print(n=3)
The select() function can be used with regular expressions to select columns based on patterns in their names.
To select columns that start with a specific string, use the starts_with() function within select().
To select columns that contain a specific string, use the contains() function within select().
The %in% operator in R is used to select rows from a data frame where a column matches one of the values in a vector.
data%>%select(short_name, dob, starts_with("skill"))%>%filter(short_name%in%c("R. Lewandowski","Cristiano Ronaldo","Neymar Jr"))%>%print(n=3)
To extract a column as a vector instead of a tibble, use the double square bracket notation [[ or the $ notation.
To extract a column as a vector using the pull() function from the dplyr package, use data%>%pull(column_name).
data%>%pull(short_name)%>%head()
To median-normalize a column, subtract the median value of the column from each data point.
The relocate() function in R is used to rearrange columns in a data frame.
To move columns to a new position, use the relocate() function with the .before or .after argument.
The filter() function enables you to choose rows that satisfy specific logical conditions.
data%>%select(short_name, dob,starts_with("skill"))%>%filter(skill_dribbling>mean(data$skill_dribbling))
The rbind() and cbind() functions add one or more rows at the end of the data frame.
data%>%select(short_name, value_eur)%>%filter(short_name=="L. Messi")%>%add_row(short_name ="P. Belej",value_eur =10000)
The where() function is primarily used to select columns based on a condition.
data[['short_name']] or data$short_name can be used to extract the column short_name from players data as a vector
The summarise() function is used to reduce data down to a single summary statistic per group, often in conjunction with group_by().

To calculate summary statistics, use the summarise() function in conjunction with group_by(), as in data%>%group_by(league_level)%>%summarise(mean_dribbling = mean(skill_dribbling), min_dribbling = min(skill_dribbling), max_dribbling = max(skill_dribbling), number_of_records = n())

To calculate the mean, minimum, maximum, and standard deviation of the skill_dribbling variable, use the following code: data%>%select(short_name, dob, skill_dribbling, league_level)%>%summarise(mean_dribbling = mean(skill_dribbling), min_dribbling = min(skill_dribbling), max_dribbling = max(skill_dribbling), number_of_records = n())
The count() function offers a straightforward way to count occurrences of unique values in one or more columns, integrating seamlessly into a tidyverse workflow.
The mutate() function is used to add new variables or modify existing ones, usually without reducing the number of rows in the dataset.
To create a new variable that is a function of existing variables, use the mutate() function, and then apply summarise() to compute a summary statistic of that new variable across different groups.
The count() function is designed for counting the occurrences of each unique combination of values in one or more columns and returns the result as a tibble.
data%>%count(preferred_foot)
The across() function in the tidyverse package allows you to apply the same transformation to a range of columns within a data frame.
data%>%select(short_name, dob, league_level,starts_with("skill"))%>%group_by(league_level)%>%summarise(across(c(skill_dribbling, skill_curve), mean,na.rm =TRUE))
The where() function is equivalent to calling a predicate function map_where() that is used to apply a function to elements of a list or vector that satisfy a given condition.
The sort() function is used to sort a vector in ascending or descending order.
sort(data$overall[1:10])
The arrange() function is used to sort data frames by one or more columns.
data%>%select(short_name, overall)%>%arrange(desc(overall))%>%head(n=5)
Tidy data is suitable for AI and ML, and is based on Codd's 3rd normal form, but with constraints framed in statistical language.
To transform wide data into long data, use the pivot_longer() function, which melts columns into key-value pairs and appends this information to other data.
The players_22.csv data can be transformed into a tidy dataset using pivot_longer(), where each column represents a variable and each row represents an observation.
To recreate the original data from a tidy dataset, use the pivot_wider() function, which is the complementary operation to pivot_longer().
In a tidy dataset, each variable must have exactly one observation, and each observation must have exactly one value for each variable.
The separate() function in the tidyr package is used to split a single character column into multiple columns based on a specified separator.
separate(data, dob, sep = '-', into = c('dob_year', 'dob_month', 'dob_day'))
The na_if() function from the dplyr package can be used to replace specified values with NA.
The drop_na() function can be used to remove rows with missing values.
The complete.cases() function returns a logical vector indicating which cases are complete, meaning they have no missing values.
Control flow in R programming refers to the order in which the program's code is executed, with primary tools being choices and loops.
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: The basic form of an if statement in R is if (condition) true_action, where if the condition is TRUE, true_action is executed, and if the condition is FALSE, the optional false_action is executed.
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: x1 <- if (TRUE) 1 else 2; x2 <- if (FALSE) 1 else 2; c(x1, x2) returns [1] 1 2
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: When using the single argument form without an else statement, if invisibly returns NULL if the condition is FALSE.

statement: The ifelse() function in R applies a test condition across each element of a vector and returns one value for TRUE elements and another for FALSE elements.

statement: skill_dribbling <- c(10, 15, 70, 35, 60); ifelse(skill_dribbling > 60, "Professional", "Amateur") returns [1] "Amateur" "Amateur" "Professional" "Amateur" "Amateur"

statement: The if_else() function from the dplyr package serves a similar purpose to ifelse() but has distinct differences for conditional data manipulation, ensuring that both TRUE and FALSE results have the same type.

statement: Data bucketing is a pre-processing technique in data analysis where continuous data is grouped into a smaller number of discrete categories, or "buckets."
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: The case_when() function in R can be used for data bucketing, as shown in the example: data%>%mutate(dribbling_class = case_when(skill_dribbling<=20~'Beginner', skill_dribbling<=40~'Amateur', skill_dribbling<=60~'Competent', skill_dribbling<=80~'Professional', TRUE~'World Class'))
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: The for loop in R is used to iterate over items in a vector, with the syntax: for(item in vector) perform_action
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: To use a for loop in R, first initialize the loop variable, then specify the vector to iterate over, and finally perform the desired action, as shown in the example: for(i in 1:3) {print(i)}
source_title: Tutorial 4.1 - Control Flow and Data Bucketing

statement: Functionals are higher-order functions that take other functions as arguments and/or return functions as results.
Loops are control flow structures that repeat a block of code as long as a specified condition is met.
Functional programming promotes immutability and stateless design, leading to more predictable and testable code.
map() creates a container for the output, applies a function to each element of a list, and fills the container with the results.
map(x, sqrt) calculates the square root of each number in the vector x.
In R, functions are objects that can be manipulated, combined, and returned as results.
The tilde ~ represents a lambda function, which ties the two parts together in map()
map(1:3, ~ rnorm(2, mean = 10, sd = 2)) generates three sets of randomly sampled data from a normal distribution.
The map() function in R is implemented in the purrr package, which is part of the tidyverse.
map() can be used to store computed values in a list, such as map(1:3, ~ runif(2))
map(data[,3:5],function(x)mean(x,na.rm =TRUE))
A data frame is essentially a list of columns.
map() deconstructs the list by extracting each column, applies mean() to each numeric vector, and then aggregates the means into a new list.
The reduce() function takes a binary function and a vector or list as arguments, and it applies the function in an iterative manner to reduce the vector to a single value.
data$skill_dribbling%>%head(n=10)%>%reduce(., sum)
In R, the concepts of infix and prefix functions refer to different ways of defining function parameters.
reduce() comes in two flavors, with the difference between using sum() inside reduce() compared to accumulate() being the way the results are accumulated.
The Grammar of Graphics provides a structured approach to data visualization, enabling the creation of complex and customized graphics through layered components.
The overall score is a comprehensive measure of a player’s abilities and performance, calculated based on various attributes such as pace, shooting, passing, dribbling, defending, and physicality.
To create a scatter plot in ggplot2, start by creating a plot object with the ggplot() function, then add aesthetic mappings and geometric objects using the aes() function.
ggplot(data = data, mapping = aes(x = skill_dribbling, y = overall))
The mapping argument of the ggplot() function defines how variables in your dataset are mapped to visual properties (aesthetics) of your plot.
The aes() function in ggplot2 specifies which variables to map to the x and y axes.
A geom is the geometrical object that a plot uses to represent data.
geom_point() adds a layer of points to a plot, creating a scatterplot.
The relationship between two numerical variables can be displayed using a scatter plot.
ggplot(data = data, mapping = aes(x = skill_dribbling, y = overall, colour = as_factor(role))) + geom_point() + scale_color_manual(values = c("#f22a2a", "#ffdbac", "grey", "#b3cde0")).
When a categorical variable is mapped to an aesthetic, ggplot2 automatically assigns a unique value of the aesthetic to each unique level of the variable.
To customize the color scheme, use scale_color_manual() to adjust the colors based on the number of unique values in the data.
Overplotting can obscure important patterns in a scatterplot.
To address overplotting, adjust the geom parameters to differentiate points for various classes by using different shapes, sizes, or transparency levels.
The size parameter adjusts the size of points, lines, or other geoms to emphasize certain data points or lines.
The shape parameter determines the shape of points in scatter plots to represent different categories or groups.
The alpha parameter controls the transparency of geoms to handle overplotting.
To tweak plot visuals, use the theme() function to adjust elements such as axis text, panel borders, and axis lines.
theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10), panel.border = element_blank(), axis.line = element_line(colour = "grey")).
To refine the plot, use the guides() function to set axis caps with guide_axis(cap = "both").
The labs() function in ggplot2 is used to improve the labels of a plot.
To move the legend to the top of the plot, set legend.position = "top" in the theme() function.
To remove the title from the legend, use legend.title = element_blank() in the theme() function.

ggplot(data = data, mapping = aes(x = skill_dribbling, y = overall, colour = as_factor(role))) + geom_point(shape = 16, alpha = 0.1, size = 2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("#f22a2a", "#ffdbac", "grey", "#b3cde0")) + labs(title = "Overall vs. Ball Dribbling", x = "Ball Dribbling", y = "Overall", color = "Role", shape = "Role") + theme(legend.position = "top", legend.title = element_blank(), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10), panel.border = element_blank(), axis.line = element_line(colour = "grey"), axis.ticks = element_line(color = "grey")) + guides(x = guide_axis(cap = "both"), y = guide_axis(cap = "both"))
The geom_smooth() function in ggplot2 is used to add a smooth curve to a plot.
To add a line of best fit to a plot, use geom_smooth(method = "lm") in ggplot2.
The {{ }} operator in ggplot2 is used to capture expressions passed to a function.

create_plot <- function(data, x, y, color_factor, plot_title) { data %>% ggplot(aes(x = {{ x }}, y = {{ y }}, colour = as_factor({{ color_factor }}), group = as_factor({{ color_factor }}))) + geom_point(shape = 16, alpha = 0.1, size = 2) + stat_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, fill = NA, linewidth = 0.6, aes(colour = as_factor({{ color_factor }}))) + scale_color_manual(values = c("#f22a2a", "#ffdbac", "grey", "#b3cde0")) + labs(title = plot_title, x = "Overall", y = "Ball Dribbling") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) + theme(legend.position = "top", legend.title = element_blank(), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10), panel.border = element_blank(), axis.line = element_line(colour = "grey"), axis.ticks = element_line(color = "grey")) + guides(x = guide_axis(cap = "both"), y = guide_axis(cap = "both")) }

patchwork is a package designed to complement ggplot2 by enabling users to combine plots with a simple and flexible syntax.

To arrange plots in a grid using patchwork, you need to wrap ggplot code into a function, create a ggplot object for each scenario, and employ specific syntax to arrange these objects into a grid structure.

The following code creates a scatter plot with specific settings: create_plot<-function(data, x, y, color_factor, color, plot_title) {data%>%ggplot(aes(x ={{ x }},y ={{ y }}))+geom_point(shape =1,size =2,colour ='lightgrey')+geom_point(data =data%>%filter(role==color_factor),shape ='circle open',size =3,color =color)+geom_point(data =data%>%filter(role==color_factor),color =color)+labs(title =plot_title,subtitle =paste('Is ball dribbling important for', color_factor,'?'),x ='Ball Dribbling',y ='Overall')+theme(legend.position='top',legend.title =element_blank(),axis.text.x =element_text(size =10),axis.text.y =element_text(size =10),panel.border =element_blank(),axis.line =element_line(colour ='grey'),axis.ticks =element_line(color ='grey'))+guides(x =guide_axis(cap ='both'),y =guide_axis(cap ='both'))}

A categorical variable is a type of variable that takes on a limited set of values.
In R, a categorical variable is referred to as a factor, a term originating from statistics.
To show the distribution of a single categorical variable, bar plots are a go-to choice, where the height of each bar shows how many observations fall into each category.
To plot the bars, you can choose between two geoms: geom_bar() or geom_col(), each offering a different approach to bar plotting.
ggplot(data, aes(x = League)) + geom_bar()
ggplot(data, aes(x = League, y = Count)) + geom_col()

The main difference between geom_bar() and geom_col() is that geom_bar() automatically counts the levels of each factor in a tibble column, while with geom_col(), you need to calculate these counts yourself using functions like tally() or count().

data %>% filter(league_name %in% c("French Ligue 1", "German 1. Bundesliga", "Spain Primera Division", "Italian Serie A", "English Premier League")) %>% count(league_name) %>% rename(League = league_name, Count = n)
To refine the plot appearance, you can highlight the bar with the highest count with a different color and add text labels to the bars showing the counts.
top.hit <- data %>% arrange(desc(Count)) %>% slice(1) %>% pull(League)

data %>% ggplot(., aes(x = reorder(League, Count), y = Count)) + geom_col(aes(fill = League == top.hit), show.legend = FALSE) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#3153a2", "FALSE" = "lightgrey"))
